sentences.text: Yes.
So, uh-- so my feet were beginning to hurt me badly.
They were beginning to get frostbitten.
And there was really nothing I could do because the people that had the huts with the heat would not let me stay with them.
And I just-- gradually, my condition became worse and worse to the point where I no longer was able-- I had such pain that I could not walk much.
And at just about that time, we were in a very large forest.
And there was sort of like, a road dividing the forest in half.
Not a paved road, but nevertheless, it was a road.
And on the other side of the road, there was another group of Jewish people hiding also-- perhaps 35 of them.
And suddenly, we got news that during the morning hours, the Ukrainians attacked that little area there, and they slaughtered most of the people there.
Maybe one or two people escaped.
And one guy that escaped knew that we were on this side of the road, deep in the woods, and he came to tell us.
He came to look for shelter, to be with people.
And he told us what happened.
And the people that- those people, the 40 people that we were close to got very concerned that they would be next.
And in the general area, as I explained to you before, most of the villages were Ukrainian, and there were Polish settlements like, between them.
There was a river.
The name of the river was the River Sluch.
And it was a big river.
And on the other side of the river, even though it was mostly populated by Ukrainians, the were more Polish religious in proportion to the amount of Polish religious on the side that we were in.
And somehow, we felt that they felt, the people, that the other side of the river is safer than being on this side of the river.
And since what had happened just to the group of people across the road, they all decided to abandon-- to go to the other side.
They also had heard rumors that on the other side of the river, the Jews are better off, and that some Jews actually walk around with rifles.
And sort of, there was a little better atmosphere.
And they decided to go.
I wasn't able to walk anymore, so they had to leave me behind.
But what they did, this time, they put me into a hut that had fire in it.
And I believe that-- I believe that they knew that the man that we are talking-- the man that is walking around with the [? sound ?] of the rifle, was my uncle.
But they didn't tell me.
So I believed that they were-- perhaps they were afraid they were going to be punished, [SOBBING] so suddenly they started to treat me nice.
So they put me into one of the huts with a fire, and actually left-- they left me some food.
And they said that as soon as they got over to the other side of the river, they will see what could be done.
Perhaps someone could come and save me.
And they left me there alone.
So I was left all alone in the woods in this hut, like a log cabin.
It was a strong log cabin with a door.

And during the night, wolves and coyotes would come, and they would scratch all night on the door, because they became brazen, you know, because there was no one to fight them off, and they could-- I guess they knew there was a living person in that place.
So they-- it was very frightening to be there alone, you know?
But I saw that they weren't able to break the door, so I sort of got used to the fact that they-- that this is every night they come, and they scratch the door.
They want to break into the log cabin that I was being housed.
But there's nothing I could do.
I couldn't walk.
And there was- they left me enough wood to feed the fire.
So I was warm, and there was a little bit of food.
So I just waited to die there or whatever.
God knows what I was thinking at the time.
And one day, a farmer came looking for wood.
I would say-- it's hard for me to remember now, but I think I would say at least 10 days.
Yes.
So after a few days, the farmer was coming to the forest to look for wood, and he realized that there were maybe four or five log cabins like that.
He realized, I guess, that they were abandoned.
So instead of chopping down wood from the trees, it was simpler to dismantle something like that.
So I could hear some noise from a distance hammering, and then he noticed that from my cabin, smoke was coming out through that little metal chimney.
So he came, and there was a little window on top for light that they built.
So he came, and he looked in, and he saw me lying.
So I could see that he was hiding his face.
I shouldn't-- I shouldn't be able to see him.
I suppose he was afraid if I was caught or something.
People were just afraid for their lives.
Even the-- the Germans did terrible things to people if they helped Jewish people to stay alive.
But the next morning, he came, and he opened the window, and he put down a bottle with milk for me.
[PAUSES] Every day, he came, and he brought me food.
[CRYING] And meanwhile, on the other side of the river, the people managed to cross the river from this side, and they all tried to find some place.
Nobody lived in a house.
Jewish people did not live in the house.
They were like animals in the woods.
But my uncle, [PAUSES], he was really an unusual person, very strong and tough guy.
And they had weapons.
So everybody had respect for him.
So he managed to live in a- to get house which was very close to the edge of the wood.
There was a house that belonged to a man that used to be a watchman for the forest.
He worked for the big companies.
God knows who owned the forest.
The people that owned the forest, he was employed by them.
And therefore, he had a home on the edge of the forest.
And my uncle-- I don't know how he-- he occupied a house, whatever it was.
But the woman, her husband was in the army.
He was a Polish man.
And she was there with a child.
So all they know, my uncle lived with this woman, with my aunt, and with one of her sons.
Because the other son was meanwhile joined the Russian partisans, because they started to appear in our area.

So the people, when they used to go around like, begging for food, they would actually come to my uncle's house because he was sort of by those standards, he was a well-to-do person, because they baked bread, they had food.
And my aunt is very nice woman, so she gave everybody whatever she had.
So this one woman came in from the side, from where I was left.
And she asked for something.
My aunt had put it on the table, and gave her some hot soup, and some bread, and they developed a conversation.
And she asked her, where you coming from?
And little by little, the woman told her she's coming from the other side.
And sort of as a side conversation, she said, oy vey, she said.
We left a little boy there.
[PAUSES]
So after my aunt was questioning, [SOBBING] she realized-- [INAUDIBLE].
[CRYING] She realized that I'm her nephew.
So she practically fainted when she heard that.
And my uncle came back.
And she said she's going to commit suicide if he doesn't go to retrieve me.
[CRYING] It was very difficult to cross-- to cross the river because the only bridge that was there was made by Germans.
And they were very careful.
They watched everybody.
So my uncle went, and he had actually forced some farmer that he knew to go across the river and to look for me.
And-- and he also heard that there was another little girl being brought up in a farmer's house as a Christian child, and he knew the parents of the girl.
So he also told the farmer to stop by over there and pick up the girl.
I don't know what threats they had to make, whatever they told him to say, but it worked.
So the farmer came, and they retrieved that girl.
And then suddenly, as I lied in my little hut, I hear somebody is calling me by my name.
So I got so excited that I-- even though I couldn't walk, I managed to run out, and I fell in the snow.
So he picked me up, and he put me in the wagon.
And during the night, we traveled to the other side of the river.
And sure enough, the one way for him to pass is through the bridge.
And that's why he was afraid to go.
But I don't know.
My uncle must have really frightened him bad enough.
And it was so cold when we came-- and this was manned by Germans, not the Ukrainian.
They had a little tiny, like a telephone booth, little house, by the bridge that the guard used to stay, and it was freezing.
So the German came out, and he just asked the farmer, what's going on?
And the farmer says-- of course, it was a wagon full of hay, and we were put on the bottom.
And they lift, put hay perhaps, six feet high.
And the farmer said he's just transporting hay for his cows.
And normally, they would take the bayonets, and they would really search.
But it was so cold that he just waved us through.
And they brought me to my aunt's house, to that house that I told you, next to the edge of the forest.
S when I came there, of course, I saw my aunt.
It was a great reunion.
And before they took me into the house, they stripped me of all my clothes, and they burned it outside, and they had clean clothes for me.
They bathed me and everything else.
And I got so excited that I asked them to put the electric on.
I forgot there was no electric, of course.
People used to-- you were lucky if you had a little kerosene.
Most of the farmers used to use wood which was saturated with some kind of substance that burns.
So they used to use that wood to light in a corner.
It was just very, very-- even the farmers had-- certain things were just not available.
And during the night, when I woke up in the morning, the smell of my feet was just-- my feet were able to thaw.
They were frozen over time.
So it was a nice warm house, and everything became normal.
And my feet were in terrible condition.
And again, my uncle, because he had the weapons, and he was a doer, a fighter, he went, and he forced the doctor, a Christian doctor, to come.
And I don't-- I'm not sure if he was Polish or Ukrainian, from some village.
And he came to examine my feet, and he said that there was-- no, he didn't come.
I'm sorry.
He gave advice.
He said we should look for pig fat and bandage my legs with the fat of a pig.
And perhaps that's the only thing that he can suggest to do that.
And they did it for a day or two.
And then, my toes were just hanging in the skin of the foot.
So my aunt took a pair of scissors, and she just-- I saw, she just cut off-- cut off my toes like that.
Didn't hurt, because everything was dead.
And after she cut off my toes, she put the bandages.
And gradually, it began to heal.
Both.
Yeah.
And it begin to heal a little bit.
Of course, it was kept clean.
It was washed every day with warm water.
And nobody had bandages, but they had materials that she washed all the time.
And kept it very clean.
I never got an infection.
And sort of things were sort of getting, for me, like I was living somewhat like a normal human being.
And then suddenly, there was a farmer came running saying that the Germans are in the village.
And since this house was removed from the village, perhaps a half a mile or something, that we had like-- there was like, a few minutes left to escape.
So in desperation, my uncle grabbed my aunt and the weapons that they had, and they ran into the woods.
And I was left in the house with the lady.
Oh, perhaps-- I don't recall.
I would say maybe a few weeks.
Maybe a month.
And uh-- and before I could turn around, I could hear there's a German officer already in the kitchen talking to the-- she lived with her daughter, a young girl.
She was a young woman, a very attractive woman too.
So when the German officer walked into the house, and he saw the woman, he had other things on his mind.
He befriended her, so to speak, or seduced her, whatever he did.

And it used to be the German policy, whenever they came into a house and someone was sick, they would kill the person and burn the house because they was always afraid of typhus, or all type of terrible diseases of that nature.
And she, not knowing what to say-- he says-- he went in and took a look at me.
And he says to her, who is this?
And she said, this is my brother.
He's sick.
But since he seduced her in the kitchen, I could hear the commotion that I suppose.

And he actually asked her-- because he spoke to her in German and a little Polish, and I could understand-- that she should come to visit him, that he was stationed in a small town on the Russian border.
And he like, on good terms.
He was very satisfied, very happy.
And he didn't kill me, didn't burn the house, and I remained.
But when my uncle returned back like, a few hours later-- once the Germans had left the village-- my uncle and my aunt returned to see what happened to me.
The woman was in such hysterics that she said that if they don't take me out of-- if they don't remove me from the house, that-- I don't know what she said she would do.
They just couldn't take it, and she was crying.
She was completely-- unbelievable.
She screamed for hours.
So my uncle didn't know what to do with me at this point.
So they had to take me out of the house on short notice, lie, and there was a place in the woods that Jewish people used to hide, say, a year ago, maybe, or eight months ago.
And the Germans already knew about this place.
They came, and the people escaped.
And they shot the whole place up with a machine gun, the building.
The building was full of bullet holes.
So out of desperation, they said they'll put me into this building for the night, just to see what-- until they decide what to do next.
And again, during the night, animals came.
Oh, they carried me on their-- they carried me on their back.
My cousin carried me on his back.
I couldn't walk.
And they took me there, and there was a bench.
And they put- they made me as comfortable as I can be.
And they closed the door, and they said that I must sleep here tonight, because the woman is in hysterics.
And she wouldn't let me stay there any longer, and they cannot force her to do anything else.
So during the night, again, animals came, and they scratched at the door, and they tried to break in.
And coyotes screamed all night long.
And if that wasn't enough, it was sort of the beginning of April, that time of the year.
And the snow-- during the night, I could hear water is coming.
The log house, or that place I stayed in, was sort of-- they took logs, and they put them against each other.
And half-- and half of the depth was dug in the ground.
So the water started coming in underneath me.
I could hear it coming and coming and coming.
I put my hand down, and I could touch the water.
But luckily for me, the bench that I was lying on was sort of perhaps two inches above the ground level.
And water seeks its own level.
So when it reached the ground, it stopped coming in.
So as a matter of fact, the next morning when my aunt came to bring me something to eat in the morning, when she opened the door-- she left the place dry.
And when she opened the door and she walked down, she got halfway into the water.
There was that much.
And after that, they took me-- they made a decision-- my uncle made a decision to go into another location altogether.
Leave this area because he thought it was a well known to the Germans that Jews are hiding there.
And again, they took me, and my cousin dragged me on his back, perhaps 10 miles distance, maybe longer.
I don't even know how long.
And we came to another location.
And the one cousin, yes.
And we came to another location, and they made a temporary hut underground because that was supposed to be just for the summer.
And little by little, my feet got slightly better.
From branches, they made me like crutches, and I was able to begin to walk around a little bit.
They took big shoes, and they put in cotton and cloth.
I was able to sort of move around a little bit, to get up, and get off my bed, and walk around.
We stayed there for about, perhaps six months.
OK.
With my uncle, there was always other people that wanted to be near him because they felt safe next to him, because he had a rifle, and his son had a rifle.
And already, the farmers had great respect for him because they were afraid of him, some of them.
So anyone that was associated with my uncle at that point was-- felt reasonably safe.
So there was always other people.
At the side of my aunt-- her name was [?
Chava too. ?] She was a very good-natured person.
So anyone-- and the word spread like fire.
So anybody that had a wound or had a problem, they came to her to be cured, to be helped.
So we were surrounded with quite a few people in that [INAUDIBLE].
There was maybe perhaps 18 to 25 people.
And even people that were further away from us, because for strategic purposes, they tried not to have all the huts in one location.
So they always used to come during the day to get some food and whatever.
My uncle always had enough food at home at that place.
So we stayed there during the entire summer, and that was already 1943.
And it was the summer of 1943.
And my uncle had made a decision to go from this place to another location, and build sort of a permanent-- not a house, like a log cabin into the mountains.
And he had came across another Jewish man which was a carpenter, and he had a mother with him.
And they agreed together to go with my cousin to build this place for us.
And the Russian underground, the partisans, were becoming more prominent.
And the war for the Germans wasn't going as well.
So I guess they didn't have that much time to run into the woods to hunt a few individual Jews that survived.
I did not have personal contact with them.
I did see them.
From time to time, they would come.
As a matter of fact, whenever they came, we had to hide our weapons, because we were always afraid that they might take it away saying that they are a military group, and they need it for themselves.
So as much as we welcomed them, we were afraid of them, that they might strip us from-- my uncle was afraid that they might take the weapons away from us.
But they were there, and there was military action against the Germans by the partisans.
And as I mentioned, the war wasn't- wasn't going that well for them any longer.
And it became sort of safer to be in the woods, because we haven't heard for a long time of any searches or attacks after that period.
So my uncle decided it was safe enough to perhaps make a permanent home for the winter.
And he went, and he built this house with this other young man and his mother.
And already at that time, my uncle owned a horse, and we had a cow that we were able to milk for ourselves.
You know?
Milk.
And we had enough food.
There was no problem, and we didn't have to go begging for food any longer.
My uncle provided, through his contacts, enough food.
And when we moved into to the new house there, before long, there was a full house of people, because everybody that showed up, my aunt had mercy on them, and she took them in.
And so we were in the house, I would say perhaps 14 people in not too long a time.
And we lived in that house until February '94, when we were liberated by the Russians.
Yes.
Yes, I'm sorry.
And when we were liberated by the Russians, we abandoned, of course, this place, and we moved into--
